2: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Globally, energy has become a priority; currently the produc- tion of renewable energy is of great relevance to most countries as a measure of energy sustainability and environmental protection. The production of clean energy from biomass is one alternative that has driven the development of new technologies and the improvement of those that have been used for many years, as is the case of anaerobic digestion for the treatment of wastew- ater and waste generated by human activities, including the livestock sector (Achinas et al., 2017). Although the latter con- tributes substantially to food security and poverty alleviation, its environmental impacts have a significant effect on air pollution, land and water degradation, and biodiversity reduction (FAO, 2009, 2016).
In particular, manure mishandling generates significant envi- ronmental impacts that threaten human and animal health, in

addition to contributing to climate change since it is a source of emissions (CH4, N20, CO2) (Perez, 2008; Hristov et al., 2013; CCAC, 2014). However, its sustainable management represents an opportunity to foster renewable energies generation and use.

Some studies have proved that energy production from animal manure is of great environmental and economic importance (Croxatto et al., 2014). Many of them focused on co-digestion that mainly utilized agricultural wastes to increase the efficiency of sys- items, in addition to incrementing biogas production, as well as energy (De Vries et al., 2012a; Lijó et al., 2014; Rodriguez-Verde et al., 2014; Cansu et al., 2016; Van Stappen et al., 2016). A number of studies also assessed the environmental impacts of mishandling manure and byproducts of the anaerobic digestion in order to eval- uate diverse techniques for reducing these impacts (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Luo et al., 2014; Paccanelli et al., 2015; Sambusiti et al., 2015; Styles et al., 2016), and improving manure manage- ment practices (Pinos-Rodríguez et al., 2012; Van Stappen et al., 2016; Zeng et al., 2016).
Anaerobic digestion (AD) of manure for the biogas and energy production is being used mainly in developed countries such as

Germany, UK, United States, and Italy, where there are modern plants on large scales (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Fusi et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Scarlat et al., 2018). In developing countries such as Mexico, India, and Vietnam, the use of this technology has also been of great interest (FIRCO, 2011; Vu et al., 2015b; Roubík et al., 2017); however, in these countries, digesters operate at different scales, the most abundant being those of small and medium scale installed in rural and semi-industrial areas. AD pro- cess in small-scale is widely reported for heat production (Surendra et al., 2014; Rupf et al., 2015; Garfi et al., 2016), but few studies at medium-scale are published (Mittal et al., 2018). For small-scale, the control of digesters show important deficien- cies due to the lack of technical knowledge and economic resources, problems that reduce the efficiency of installed systems (Bond and Templeton, 2011), which could generate greater envi- ronmental impacts; it has been reported that these digesters gen- erate more greenhouse gases emissions (Bruun et al., 2014; Roubík et al., 2016). There are still discharges to rivers and soils; moreover, biogas flaring is the most viable option to avoid intentional emis- sion when an excess of biogas is produced in the digesters (Bruun et al., 2014; Vu al., 2015b).

To date, Mexico promotes, through economic incentives, the installation of digesters in livestock farms (FIRCO, 2011). Despite governmental and private support for this endeavor, the installed capacity is not used fully for energy production, and therefore, it is a common practice flaring some of the biogas generated, and using part of the manure for composting or spreading it directly in crop areas. Only 31% of medium-scale digestion systems installed in the industrial sector in Mexico use biogas for energy production, the remaining 69% is flared (IRRIMEXICO, 2015), with- out taking advantage of its calorific capacity. This kind of practices facilitates the manure management due to the lack of technical capabilities and economic resources in the rural areas (FIRCO, 2011).

The study aims to assess through a Life Cycle Analysis (LCA), the environmental effects of the energy production from pig manure treatment by anaerobic digestion at medium-scale. It also com- pared common practices of biogas flaring and conventional man- agement in order to quantify environmental benefits from implementing energy-sustainability programs on renewable ener- gies, based on biomass and biogas production.

6: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a methodology associated with the entire life cycle of a product or process. The LCA objective in the study is to determine the potential environmental effects of the energy production and the practice of biogas flaring, during the treatment of pig manure by anaerobic digestion at medium- scale. To this end, a case study in a pig farm in Puebla, Mexico, was evaluated. The studied system reflects the main types of pig manure treatment and management practices used in Mexico and were selected based on official information reported by the Mexican Government and International Agencies (FIRCO, 2011; IRRIMEXICO, 2015).
7: <header>2.2. Functional unit and system boundaries </header>

The functional unit (FU) selected to carry out the assessment was one ton of manure produced in the farm (solid and liquid man- ure). LCA is limited to the treatment and handling of liquid and solid manure generated in the farm. The system does not include animal production, feeding, nor the construction and dismantling

of the equipment used. The inputs are the manure generated, energy (electricity and fuels), and water. The main outputs are bio- gas and electricity, compost and treated water for irrigation (Fig. 1).
8: <header>2.3. Site description </header>

The site is a porcine farm located in the State of Puebla in Mex- ico, with an annual average production of 5000 head of cattle. Pigs generate liquid and solid manure; the latter is dry material that is scraped from the floors and sent to storage in the open for further treatment by composting. Liquid manure are washed with water and stored in open systems. The AD process is carried out under a semi-continuous system in two covered anaerobic lagoons of 2630 m³ of capacity each one. The mixing inside the lagoon is done every week by means of a pumping system with double pipes at different depths. The digesters have a flow of 16 /day, with a hydraulic retention time (HRT) of 162 days, an organic load at the entrance of 0.16 kg SVT/m³/ and a biogas yield of 1.2 m³/ kg SVT.

Biogas is filtered for the H2S elimination before being trans- formed to energy. The energy generators produce between 30 and 35 effective kWh with an efficiency of 1 kWh/m³ of biogas. The biogas transformation to energy is limited by the internal demand of the farm, including the electricity requirements of man- ure treatment. The surplus biogas is sent directly to the burner without going through the H2S filter. The AD process generates two byproducts: digestate and a liquid effluent, these byproducts are treated separately. The digestate is partially dehydrated, and then it is mixed with the dry manure and sent to composting treat- ment. Weekly, 7.5 tons of compost are generated and transported to fields near to the farm, as an organic fertilizer. The liquid effluent is treated in sedimentation lagoons, aeration tanks and a bamboo wetland and it is reused in irrigation.

10: The LCA was carried out in four systems: two systems with dif- ferent energy production and two systems without energy produc- tion. Based on the process description, the four systems were divided into six subsystems to facilitate the analysis: (1) separation of liquid and solid manure, (2) storage of solid manure and washed liquid manure, (3) anaerobic digestion process, (4) treatment of intermediate products, (5) use of products, and (6) avoided prod- ucts. The four systems evaluated are shown in Fig. 1 and described as follow:

Partial energy production (S1). This system represents the oper- ation conditions of the characterized farm, evaluates partial energy production, which is limited by the internal demand. In this sys- tem, 59% (base ST) of manure is treated by anaerobic digestion and 41% (base ST) by composting. Electricity is provided for the energy produced in the system, so it is not considered energy from the grid. A 10% of biogas burning was considered for this system according information provided by the owner.

Total energy production (S2). The system is a simulated scenario with the same energy efficiency and performance of the character- ized process. In this system, energy is produced by the treatment of 100% of the manure (liquid and solid). Electricity produced for the system is used for internal consumption and surpluses could be distributed in the national network. A minimum percentage of 1% biogas burning was established. This system generates compost and considers the reuse of treated water in the process.

11: The LCA was carried out in four systems: two systems with dif- ferent energy production and two systems without energy produc- tion. Based on the process description, the four systems were divided into six subsystems to facilitate the analysis: (1) separation of liquid and solid manure, (2) storage of solid manure and washed liquid manure, (3) anaerobic digestion process, (4) treatment of intermediate products, (5) use of products, and (6) avoided prod- ucts. The four systems evaluated are shown in Fig. 1 and described as follow:

Partial energy production (S1). This system represents the oper- ation conditions of the characterized farm, evaluates partial energy production, which is limited by the internal demand. In this sys- tem, 59% (base ST) of manure is treated by anaerobic digestion and 41% (base ST) by composting. Electricity is provided for the energy produced in the system, so it is not considered energy from the grid. A 10% of biogas burning was considered for this system according information provided by the owner.

Total energy production (S2). The system is a simulated scenario with the same energy efficiency and performance of the character- ized process. In this system, energy is produced by the treatment of 100% of the manure (liquid and solid). Electricity produced for the system is used for internal consumption and surpluses could be distributed in the national network. A minimum percentage of 1% biogas burning was established. This system generates compost and considers the reuse of treated water in the process.

15: Matter and energy flows were obtained with field and labora- tory data during the characterization of the selected site, and with information provided by the owner of the farm between 2015 and 2016. Uncharacterized systems were simulated from information and process data from the study site.

In the laboratory, the composition of washed liquid and solid manure, intermediate products and byproducts were determined by quantifying total solids (TS) and total volatile solids (TVS), using a gravimetric method. For estimating the total nitrogen (TN), an Elemental Costech ECS 4010 analyzer was utilized. The composi- tion of total manure, liquid manure and semidry digestate were estimated by mass balance.

The S3 system was simulated using the same data from S1 sys- tem. For the S2 and S4 systems, the flows and composition of the intermediate and final products and byproducts were estimated by mass balance from the characterized system (S1).

The four systems evaluated use the same inputs. Total manure has a flow of 2807 tons/year, which is divided into two streams: solid manure at a flow of 337 tons/year and washed liquid manure of 11,339 ton/year (Table 1). The biogas production is the main product of systems S1, S2 and S3; anaerobic digestion is not used in the S4 system. The composition of biogas was quantified in the laboratory using a gas chromatography analysis in samples obtained in the farm before H2S filtration. The gas composition was 75.6% of CH4, 24.4% of CO2, and 1463 ppm of H2S.

The biogas production in S1 was of 335,946 /year, and in the S2 system of 579,809 m³/year (Table 2). For the S3 system, the same biogas production of S1 was considered. A percentage of bio- gas produced is eliminated by flaring, unintentional losses and released during the digestate discharge, but the greater percentage

is transformed into energy. These percentages were selected based on a review of scientific literature and data provided by the owner of the farm. Biogas flaring, in S1 system, was considered 10% of the total, in the S2 a minimum percentage of 1% was used, while in the S3 biogas flaring is the most important activity with 85% of the total biogas produced. In the three systems, 5% of unintentional biogas losses were considered (Lijó et al., 2014; Rodriguez-Verde et al., 2014; Vu et al., 2015b). In addition, a 10% as result of diges- tate discharge and its potential of methane production was included (UNFCCC/CCNUCC, 2012).

The S1 system has an energy consumption of 85,081 kWh/year and generates 251,959 kWh/year from the biogas production (Table 3); the produced energy could be used to supply the con- sumption of the system itself (34%) and other internal demands of the farm (66%). In general, the farm has an electricity consump- tion of 336,822 kWh/year, of which 75% is supplied from the sys- tem and 25% from the network.

For the S2 system was estimated a greater amount of electrical energy from biogas of 487,040 kWh/year, but it also has a higher electricity consumption of 172,140 kWh/year. The 87% of the energy generated for this system could be used to supply the elec- tricity consumption in the farm and 13% of surplus for distribution in the national network (63,159 kWh/year). The S3 system has the same electricity consumption as S1, but in this case, the process does not generate energy, so the network supplies 100% of the energy consumption. For the S4 system was estimated an electric- ity consumption of 19,872 kWh/year for the manure management, which is obtained from the grid.

18: Matter and energy flows were obtained with field and labora- tory data during the characterization of the selected site, and with information provided by the owner of the farm between 2015 and 2016. Uncharacterized systems were simulated from information and process data from the study site.

In the laboratory, the composition of washed liquid and solid manure, intermediate products and byproducts were determined by quantifying total solids (TS) and total volatile solids (TVS), using a gravimetric method. For estimating the total nitrogen (TN), an Elemental Costech ECS 4010 analyzer was utilized. The composi- tion of total manure, liquid manure and semidry digestate were estimated by mass balance.

The S3 system was simulated using the same data from S1 sys- tem. For the S2 and S4 systems, the flows and composition of the intermediate and final products and byproducts were estimated by mass balance from the characterized system (S1).

The four systems evaluated use the same inputs. Total manure has a flow of 2807 tons/year, which is divided into two streams: solid manure at a flow of 337 tons/year and washed liquid manure of 11,339 ton/year (Table 1). The biogas production is the main product of systems S1, S2 and S3; anaerobic digestion is not used in the S4 system. The composition of biogas was quantified in the laboratory using a gas chromatography analysis in samples obtained in the farm before H2S filtration. The gas composition was 75.6% of CH4, 24.4% of CO2, and 1463 ppm of H2S.

The biogas production in S1 was of 335,946 /year, and in the S2 system of 579,809 m³/year (Table 2). For the S3 system, the same biogas production of S1 was considered. A percentage of bio- gas produced is eliminated by flaring, unintentional losses and released during the digestate discharge, but the greater percentage

is transformed into energy. These percentages were selected based on a review of scientific literature and data provided by the owner of the farm. Biogas flaring, in S1 system, was considered 10% of the total, in the S2 a minimum percentage of 1% was used, while in the S3 biogas flaring is the most important activity with 85% of the total biogas produced. In the three systems, 5% of unintentional biogas losses were considered (Lijó et al., 2014; Rodriguez-Verde et al., 2014; Vu et al., 2015b). In addition, a 10% as result of diges- tate discharge and its potential of methane production was included (UNFCCC/CCNUCC, 2012).

The S1 system has an energy consumption of 85,081 kWh/year and generates 251,959 kWh/year from the biogas production (Table 3); the produced energy could be used to supply the con- sumption of the system itself (34%) and other internal demands of the farm (66%). In general, the farm has an electricity consump- tion of 336,822 kWh/year, of which 75% is supplied from the sys- tem and 25% from the network.

For the S2 system was estimated a greater amount of electrical energy from biogas of 487,040 kWh/year, but it also has a higher electricity consumption of 172,140 kWh/year. The 87% of the energy generated for this system could be used to supply the elec- tricity consumption in the farm and 13% of surplus for distribution in the national network (63,159 kWh/year). The S3 system has the same electricity consumption as S1, but in this case, the process does not generate energy, so the network supplies 100% of the energy consumption. For the S4 system was estimated an electric- ity consumption of 19,872 kWh/year for the manure management, which is obtained from the grid.

by carbon and nitrogen balance with data obtained from this study. For these factors, 97% of emitted carbon in the CO2 form and 3% in CH4 form were considered, while for nitrogen emissions 1.5% of total nitrogen, emitted as N20 was used (Basset-Mens et al., 2007; Vu et al., 2015a). For NH3 emission, the average ratio of 0.725 N-NH3/NT of a range from 0.24 to 0.93 N-NH3/NT was applied based on Wang et al. (2017).

For the biogas flaring, the emission factors were calculated from the biogas composition of CO2 and SO2, considering a complete combustion of gas. For the calculation of emissions factors for com- post and manure transport were considered fossil fuels and trucks with a capacity of 8-10 Trucks consume 2.5 L of diesel/km
(CAPUFE, 2004) generating 3145 kg CO2/kg of diesel (INECC, 2014). For this study, 2 trucks per week with round trips of 20 km each one were considered.

The N20 and NH3 are the most important emissions during compost, and in solid and liquid manure application on agricultural fields. For solid and liquid manure, the emissions factors reported by Wang et al. (2017) were used (see Table 4). For compost appli- cation, NH3 emissions of 50% of ammoniacal nitrogen were applied (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

In order to calculate avoided emissions for the substitution of inorganic fertilizers, substitution for the same quantity of nitrogen in compost and manure was assumed. The emission factors reported by the IPCC (2006b) for N20 of 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg TN and by Vu et al. (2015a) for NH3 of 0.24 kg NH3-N/kg TN were used.

was not considered as an input in the inventory. For the S3 and S4 systems, energy is supplied from the grid.

The LCA was carried out by using the SIMAPRO pHD 8.1.1.16/2016 software. Electricity, diesel and water inputs were obtained from Ecoinvent 3 database. In the case of electricity pro- duction, the high voltage production mix in Mexico was used at the industrial level (>24 k\ with data from 2012 to 2014 that includes the technologies for the production and distribution of energy. Due to the lack of data for Mexico related to water extrac- tion, it was used the Quebec's (2012-2014) database for ground- water extraction without treatment. For the consumption of diesel, a refining process was used from crude oil with data from Europe (without Switzerland).

To estimate the avoided environmental impacts by the produc- tion of substituted inorganic fertilizers, a mixture of two com- pounds and processes was utilized: 50% ammonium sulfate and 50% urea. Percentages were selected based on information for Mex- ico, where fertilizers are supplied by national production of ammo- nium sulfate, and imported urea (ANACOFER, 2011; ASERCA, 2013). Databases for the production of the two fertilizers are reported for Europe (RER), with average data from various plants. The amount of substituted inorganic fertilizer was estimated tak- ing into consideration the same amount of N in compost and man- ure. The study took into account biogenic CO2, only fossil CO2 emissions generated by diesel consumption were registered; all CH4 emissions were considered non-biogenic.

a Storage volume of 150 m³ for S1, S2, S3. b Lagoon volume of 2630 m³ for S4. C 80% of TN as NH4 (Croxatto et al., 2014). d 74.5% of OM degraded. e 62% of TN converted. f 0.725 N-NH3/NT (Wang et al., 2017). g 25% TAN/TN (Brockmann et al., 2014).

by carbon and nitrogen balance with data obtained from this study. For these factors, 97% of emitted carbon in the CO2 form and 3% in CH4 form were considered, while for nitrogen emissions 1.5% of total nitrogen, emitted as N20 was used (Basset-Mens et al., 2007; Vu et al., 2015a). For NH3 emission, the average ratio of 0.725 N-NH3/NT of a range from 0.24 to 0.93 N-NH3/NT was applied based on Wang et al. (2017).

For the biogas flaring, the emission factors were calculated from the biogas composition of CO2 and SO2, considering a complete combustion of gas. For the calculation of emissions factors for com- post and manure transport were considered fossil fuels and trucks with a capacity of 8-10 Trucks consume 2.5 L of diesel/km
(CAPUFE, 2004) generating 3145 kg CO2/kg of diesel (INECC, 2014). For this study, 2 trucks per week with round trips of 20 km each one were considered.

The N20 and NH3 are the most important emissions during compost, and in solid and liquid manure application on agricultural fields. For solid and liquid manure, the emissions factors reported by Wang et al. (2017) were used (see Table 4). For compost appli- cation, NH3 emissions of 50% of ammoniacal nitrogen were applied (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

In order to calculate avoided emissions for the substitution of inorganic fertilizers, substitution for the same quantity of nitrogen in compost and manure was assumed. The emission factors reported by the IPCC (2006b) for N20 of 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg TN and by Vu et al. (2015a) for NH3 of 0.24 kg NH3-N/kg TN were used.

was not considered as an input in the inventory. For the S3 and S4 systems, energy is supplied from the grid.

The LCA was carried out by using the SIMAPRO pHD 8.1.1.16/2016 software. Electricity, diesel and water inputs were obtained from Ecoinvent 3 database. In the case of electricity pro- duction, the high voltage production mix in Mexico was used at the industrial level (>24 k\ with data from 2012 to 2014 that includes the technologies for the production and distribution of energy. Due to the lack of data for Mexico related to water extrac- tion, it was used the Quebec's (2012-2014) database for ground- water extraction without treatment. For the consumption of diesel, a refining process was used from crude oil with data from Europe (without Switzerland).

To estimate the avoided environmental impacts by the produc- tion of substituted inorganic fertilizers, a mixture of two com- pounds and processes was utilized: 50% ammonium sulfate and 50% urea. Percentages were selected based on information for Mex- ico, where fertilizers are supplied by national production of ammo- nium sulfate, and imported urea (ANACOFER, 2011; ASERCA, 2013). Databases for the production of the two fertilizers are reported for Europe (RER), with average data from various plants. The amount of substituted inorganic fertilizer was estimated tak- ing into consideration the same amount of N in compost and man- ure. The study took into account biogenic CO2, only fossil CO2 emissions generated by diesel consumption were registered; all CH4 emissions were considered non-biogenic.

For NH3 emission, the average ratio of 0.725 N-NH3/NT of a range from 0.24 to 0.93 N-NH3/NT was applied based on Wang et al. (2017).

For the biogas flaring, the emission factors were calculated from the biogas composition of CO2 and SO2, considering a complete combustion of gas. For the calculation of emissions factors for com- post and manure transport were considered fossil fuels and trucks with a capacity of 8-10 Trucks consume 2.5 L of diesel/km
(CAPUFE, 2004) generating 3145 kg CO2/kg of diesel (INECC, 2014). For this study, 2 trucks per week with round trips of 20 km each one were considered.

The N20 and NH3 are the most important emissions during compost, and in solid and liquid manure application on agricultural fields. For solid and liquid manure, the emissions factors reported by Wang et al. (2017) were used (see Table 4). For compost appli- cation, NH3 emissions of 50% of ammoniacal nitrogen were applied (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

In order to calculate avoided emissions for the substitution of inorganic fertilizers, substitution for the same quantity of nitrogen in compost and manure was assumed. The emission factors reported by the IPCC (2006b) for N20 of 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg TN and by Vu et al. (2015a) for NH3 of 0.24 kg NH3-N/kg TN were used.

was not considered as an input in the inventory. For the S3 and S4 systems, energy is supplied from the grid.

The LCA was carried out by using the SIMAPRO pHD 8.1.1.16/2016 software. Electricity, diesel and water inputs were obtained from Ecoinvent 3 database. In the case of electricity pro- duction, the high voltage production mix in Mexico was used at the industrial level (>24 k\ with data from 2012 to 2014 that includes the technologies for the production and distribution of energy. Due to the lack of data for Mexico related to water extrac- tion, it was used the Quebec's (2012-2014) database for ground- water extraction without treatment. For the consumption of diesel, a refining process was used from crude oil with data from Europe (without Switzerland).

To estimate the avoided environmental impacts by the produc- tion of substituted inorganic fertilizers, a mixture of two com- pounds and processes was utilized: 50% ammonium sulfate and 50% urea. Percentages were selected based on information for Mex- ico, where fertilizers are supplied by national production of ammo- nium sulfate, and imported urea (ANACOFER, 2011; ASERCA, 2013). Databases for the production of the two fertilizers are reported for Europe (RER), with average data from various plants. The amount of substituted inorganic fertilizer was estimated tak- ing into consideration the same amount of N in compost and man- ure. The study took into account biogenic CO2, only fossil CO2 emissions generated by diesel consumption were registered; all CH4 emissions were considered non-biogenic.

The LCA was carried out by using the SIMAPRO pHD 8.1.1.16/2016 software. Electricity, diesel and water inputs were obtained from Ecoinvent 3 database. In the case of electricity pro- duction, the high voltage production mix in Mexico was used at the industrial level (>24 k\ with data from 2012 to 2014 that includes the technologies for the production and distribution of energy. Due to the lack of data for Mexico related to water extrac- tion, it was used the Quebec's (2012-2014) database for ground- water extraction without treatment. For the consumption of diesel, a refining process was used from crude oil with data from Europe (without Switzerland).

To estimate the avoided environmental impacts by the produc- tion of substituted inorganic fertilizers, a mixture of two com- pounds and processes was utilized: 50% ammonium sulfate and 50% urea. Percentages were selected based on information for Mex- ico, where fertilizers are supplied by national production of ammo- nium sulfate, and imported urea (ANACOFER, 2011; ASERCA, 2013). Databases for the production of the two fertilizers are reported for Europe (RER), with average data from various plants. The amount of substituted inorganic fertilizer was estimated tak- ing into consideration the same amount of N in compost and man- ure. The study took into account biogenic CO2, only fossil CO2 emissions generated by diesel consumption were registered; all CH4 emissions were considered non-biogenic.

by carbon and nitrogen balance with data obtained from this study. For these factors, 97% of emitted carbon in the CO2 form and 3% in CH4 form were considered, while for nitrogen emissions 1.5% of total nitrogen, emitted as N20 was used (Basset-Mens et al., 2007; Vu et al., 2015a). For NH3 emission, the average ratio of 0.725 N-NH3/NT of a range from 0.24 to 0.93 N-NH3/NT was applied based on Wang et al. (2017).

For the biogas flaring, the emission factors were calculated from the biogas composition of CO2 and SO2, considering a complete combustion of gas. For the calculation of emissions factors for com- post and manure transport were considered fossil fuels and trucks with a capacity of 8-10 Trucks consume 2.5 L of diesel/km
(CAPUFE, 2004) generating 3145 kg CO2/kg of diesel (INECC, 2014). For this study, 2 trucks per week with round trips of 20 km each one were considered.

The N20 and NH3 are the most important emissions during compost, and in solid and liquid manure application on agricultural fields. For solid and liquid manure, the emissions factors reported by Wang et al. (2017) were used (see Table 4). For compost appli- cation, NH3 emissions of 50% of ammoniacal nitrogen were applied (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

In order to calculate avoided emissions for the substitution of inorganic fertilizers, substitution for the same quantity of nitrogen in compost and manure was assumed. The emission factors reported by the IPCC (2006b) for N20 of 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg TN and by Vu et al. (2015a) for NH3 of 0.24 kg NH3-N/kg TN were used.

The LCA was carried out by using the SIMAPRO pHD 8.1.1.16/2016 software. Electricity, diesel and water inputs were obtained from Ecoinvent 3 database. In the case of electricity pro- duction, the high voltage production mix in Mexico was used at the industrial level (>24 k\ with data from 2012 to 2014 that includes the technologies for the production and distribution of energy. Due to the lack of data for Mexico related to water extrac- tion, it was used the Quebec's (2012-2014) database for ground- water extraction without treatment. For the consumption of diesel, a refining process was used from crude oil with data from Europe (without Switzerland).

To estimate the avoided environmental impacts by the produc- tion of substituted inorganic fertilizers, a mixture of two com- pounds and processes was utilized: 50% ammonium sulfate and 50% urea. Percentages were selected based on information for Mex- ico, where fertilizers are supplied by national production of ammo- nium sulfate, and imported urea (ANACOFER, 2011; ASERCA, 2013). Databases for the production of the two fertilizers are reported for Europe (RER), with average data from various plants. The amount of substituted inorganic fertilizer was estimated tak- ing into consideration the same amount of N in compost and man- ure. The study took into account biogenic CO2, only fossil CO2 emissions generated by diesel consumption were registered; all CH4 emissions were considered non-biogenic.
11: The Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) was carried out at the midpoint level using 2013 CML-IA method (version 4.2), developed by the Center for Environmental Sciences of the University of Lei- den, Netherlands. The results considered the total impacts gener- ated directly by the manure treatment and the avoided impacts by the substitution of energy from fossil sources and inorganic fer- tilizers. The net impacts were calculated by the difference of the total impacts (positive values) minus the avoided impacts (nega- tive values). Net impacts with positive values were considered impacts or environmental charges, and negative values were con- sidered benefits or environmental credits.
12: A sensitivity analysis was conducted varying the percentages of non-intentional biogas emissions during anaerobic digestion and emissions during digestate storage and dehydration. The following two scenarios with low and high emission factors were evaluated in systems S1, S2, and S3: (1) low values with 1% unintentional emissions and 5% losses in the digestate, and (2) high values of 10% of emissions and 20% of losses in the digestate. To perform the sensitivity analysis in the conventional system, a single sce- nario was evaluated in which the discharge to water bodies was eliminated, this implies the storage of all the diluted residues in the uncovered lagoons prior its use in the crop fields.
13: The environmental impacts on climate change (CC) of the 4 sys- tems are detailed in Fig. 2a. The system with partial energy produc- tion and composting (S1) generated net impacts of 272 kg CO2eq and the system with the highest energy production (S2) emitted 359 kg CO2eq. These impacts were associated to the digestate dis- charge and the unintentional releases, which represented 46% and

23% respectively. The most important avoided impacts in both sys- tems were due to the substitution of electricity consumption from fossil sources (82% and 93%); meanwhile inorganic fertilizers sub- stitution had a lower contribution. The S2 system had the highest avoided impacts of 104 kg CO2eq; however this was not enough to reduce the impacts below S1. Both systems had a difference of 87 kg CO2eq. This indicated that producing 1.9 fold more energy generated 32% more environmental impacts.

The S3 system, which represents the biogas flaring, produced total impacts of 356 kg CO2eq, slightly higher than the partial energy production system (S1); the difference is explained by the electricity consumption of the grid in the S3. When avoided emis- sions are considered, the difference between both systems increases (72 kg CO2eq) due to biogas flaring instead of producing energy: the net emissions of the S3 were 344 kg CO2eq. The biogas flaring represents an increase of 26% on CC impacts.

The conventional S4 system generated the highest net impacts of 402 CO2eq. In this system, the storage of liquid manure in uncovered lagoons contributed with 89% of the total impacts, while the storage of dry manure contributed only with a 10%. Between the S1 and S4 systems, there is a difference of 130 kg CO2- eq, which is equivalent to an increase of 48% impacts if the manure is not treated. These highlights show the importance of applying treatments for reducing the impacts on global warming. Prapaspongsa et al. (2010) found similar results: a reduction of 144-147 kg CO2eq per ton of pig manure in an anaerobic digestion system with energy recovery.

The net impacts for the 4 systems evaluated were between 270 and 402 kg of CO2 eq per ton of manure. In the study, high values per ton of manure were obtained compared to other studies that report negative or low values per ton of substrate; this difference could be explained because leaks or handling by-products were not considered or low percentages of fugitive emissions were used (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; De Vries et al., 2012a; Croxatto et al., 2014).

16: Four systems had a similar behavior on photochemical oxida- tion (PO) to that observed for CC (Fig. 2b). The S1 system had impacts of 0.056 kg ethylene eq impacts were also due to the byproducts releases from anaerobic digester (50%) and methane emission by leaks (25%). The S2 system decreased its impacts by 30%, reaching 0.075 kg ethylene eq; releases during AD have the highest percentages of total impacts. Avoided impacts for systems with energy production (S1 and S2) come mainly from the replace- ment of the electricity from the network (92-97%).

The S3 and S4 systems had net impacts of 0.095 and 0.098 kg ethylene eq respectively. These differences between the S3 and S1 systems were explained due to the SO2 emissions from biogas flaring; and for the conventional system (S4), they were caused by, CH4 emissions into the atmosphere during liquid manure storage.

Both results on CC and PO pointed out that the greatest impacts were generated by unintentional leaks in the digester and during the discharge of the digestate. These findings have also been reported in other articles (Lijó et al., 2014; Van Stappen et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016).

18: The effects of the emissions generated by eutrophication (Eu) are shown in Fig. 2c. In general, the greatest contribution comes from the manure storage prior to its treatment or handling, with approximately 50% of the impacts. Composting is the second pro- cess that contributes significantly, followed manure application

on crop fields. Systems with treatment (S1, S2 and S3) had very similar net impacts, although the total energy production system (S2) had slightly lower values of 2.21 kg PO4 eq due to the lower compost production. This reduces NH3 emissions compared to the S1 system with 2.25 kg PO4 eq, while the S3 system registered impacts of 2.33 kg of PO4eq. For this category, avoided impacts come mainly from the substitution of inorganic fertilizers.

According to the results of the S1 and S2 systems, composting process generates eutrophication impacts; however, they promote environmental improvements on CC and PO. It is known that com- posting is a simple and economic treatment with relevant environ- mental benefits (Dennehy et al., 2017; Sáez et al., 2017), but studies have reported greater impacts compared to other manage- ment techniques (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

The conventional S4 system had the greatest impacts on Eu, which were generated by the discharge of manure into the river with high levels of N and organic matter, contributing with 69% of the impacts; the remaining 19% and 7% were related to the manure storage and its use on crop fields, respectively. In this study, the phosphorus content was not ana- lyzed; however, its contribution is of great importance (Luo et al., 2014). Although the S4 system had the highest avoided impacts of 0.707 kg of PO4eq, the discharge to water bodies generates high environmental loads, so this system obtained net impacts of 5.97kg of PO4eq, more than double compared to the systems that apply treatment. The discharge of waste to soils and bodies of water with high content of nutrients and organic matter is a practice reported in few studies because it is an inadequate practice of manure handling exc- reta; nevertheless, in developing countries it is still a common practice (Luo et al., 2014; Vu et al., 2015b).

Composting is the second pro- cess that contributes significantly, followed manure application

Fig. 2. Environmental total impacts for the life cycle of one ton of pig manure in four management systems. S1 = partial energy production; S2 = total energy production; S3 = biogas flaring, and S4 = conventional management.

on crop fields. Systems with treatment (S1, S2 and S3) had very similar net impacts, although the total energy production system (S2) had slightly lower values of 2.21 kg PO4 eq due to the lower compost production. This reduces NH3 emissions compared to the S1 system with 2.25 kg PO4 eq, while the S3 system registered impacts of 2.33 kg of PO4eq. For this category, avoided impacts come mainly from the substitution of inorganic fertilizers.

According to the results of the S1 and S2 systems, composting process generates eutrophication impacts; however, they promote environmental improvements on CC and PO. It is known that com- posting is a simple and economic treatment with relevant environ- mental benefits (Dennehy et al., 2017; Sáez et al., 2017), but studies have reported greater impacts compared to other manage- ment techniques (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

The conventional S4 system had the greatest impacts on Eu, which were generated by the discharge of manure into the river with high levels of N and organic matter, contributing with 69% of the impacts; the remaining 19% and 7% were related to the manure storage and its use on crop fields, respectively. In this study, the phosphorus content was not ana- lyzed; however, its contribution is of great importance (Luo et al., 2014). Although the S4 system had the highest avoided impacts of 0.707 kg of PO4eq, the discharge to water bodies generates high environmental loads, so this system obtained net impacts of 5.97kg of PO4eq, more than double compared to the systems that apply treatment. The discharge of waste to soils and bodies of water with high content of nutrients and organic matter is a practice reported in few studies because it is an inadequate practice of manure handling exc- reta; nevertheless, in developing countries it is still a common practice (Luo et al., 2014; Vu et al., 2015b).
22: The total impacts on acidification (Ac) in the three systems with treatment (S1, S2 and S3) were the result of the NH3 emis- sions during the storage (48-52%), the composting (22-32%), the drying of the digestate (13-23%), and the application of compost as an organic fertilizer in the fields (2.4-3.4%) (Fig. 2d). Other studies have also found that the storage of liquid and solid man- ure is one of the main practices that have shown impacts on eutrophication and acidification (Paccanelli et al., 2015; Hoeve et al., 2014).

The systems with energy production (S1 and S2) presented net impacts of 10.1 and 9.8 kg SO2 eq, respectively, while the biogas flaring system (S3) showed higher impacts of 11.06 kg SO2 eq due to a higher production of compost and SO2 emissions during the biogas burning. For the S1 system, avoided impacts are mainly generated by the substitution of inorganic fertilizers (66%), while in the S2 only 40% comes from the substitution of fertilizers and 60% from the fossil energy substitution. The system without treat- ment (S4) had the lowest impacts (6.36 kg SO2 eq) because of the greater substitution of inorganic fertilizers, since the manure stor- age contributed with the highest percentages (79%).

Manure application on crop fields contributed with 21% of impacts on Ac, this practice had greater impacts of 1.98 kg SO2 eq than compost application or other treatments whose contribu- tion were from 0.26 to 0.38 kg SO2 eq. Overall, this study did not consider the use of digestate as organic fertilizers, but it took into account the use of manure in agricultural soils. Currently, the use of these wastes as organic fertilizers is not clear; there is not scientific consensus on their potential risks of pollution. The use of digestate has great bene- fits by avoiding the use of inorganic fertilizers, however environ- mental problems, such as over fertilization, have been found during its use in crop fields (Zeng et al., 2016; Pinos-Rodríguez et al., 2012; Van Stappen et al., 2016); therefore, digestate pre- treatment is suggested to be implemented to avoid pollution (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015; Styles et al., 2016).

On the other hand, the use of manure as organic fertilizer is a conventional practice still used in various countries (Dennehy et al., 2017); even though over fertilization and pollution related to emissions of odorous compounds, microbes, antibiotics, hor- mones and heavy metals, have been reported (Salazar et al., 2003; Hoeve et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012b). In Mexico, the use of manure as fertilizers is a common practice and its manage- ment is promoted under a sustainability approach to take advan- tage of its nutritional potential in agricultural soils (SAGARPA- SENASICA, 2004; Domínguez-Araujo et al., 2014).

26: The two systems with energy production (S1 and S2) exhibited, during the process, the lowest impacts on fossil resource depletion (FRD), because they do not use electricity from the network; the impacts recorded were generated by the use of diesel during com- posting (82%) and compost transport to crop fields (18%) (Fig. 2e).

In this category, impacts avoided by the substitution of energy from fossil sources were more important than those avoided by the substitution of inorganic fertilizers. For the S1 and S2 systems avoided impacts were 917 and 1662 MJ, generating negative net impacts of -863 and -1608 respectively, which represent environmental benefits. In contrast of the results found in the study, Vu et al. (2015a, 2015b) reported that the substitution of mineral fertilizers saves more fossil resources than the same avoided production of non-renewable energy consumption; how- ever, it is important to consider that this depends on the amount of biogas produced in the system.

The S3 system, which does not produce energy, requires the electricity consumption from the grid, as well as the consumption of diesel for composting and compost transport, generating greater depletion of fossil resources. The impacts avoided in this system were of 88 MJ, registering net impacts of 246 the highest of the four systems evaluated; this highlights the environmental bur- den of using fossil energy.

The conventional system (S4) had a low energy consumption of the network, mainly due to manure washing (47%) as well as its transportation and application on crop fields (38%). The impacts avoided by the substitution of inorganic fertilizers were higher (of 247 MJ) than in the other systems, because of a greater quantity of manure applied in the fields as organic fertilizer. The impacts for the S4 system decreased to -134 MJ, being greater than the net impacts in the systems with energy production (S1 and S2).

28: The abiotic resources depletion (ARD) is a category of impact that refers to the consumption of non-biological resources such as minerals, metals and water. During the manure treatment sig- nificant quantities of water are required for manure washing and AD process, while minerals and metals are used for electricity, die- sel and inorganic fertilizer production.

Composting and the transport and application of compost have the greatest impacts during the process; thus, avoiding the con- sumption of inorganic fertilizers represents great environmental benefits. The substitution of inorganic fertilizers contributed with 60% and 80% of the total of avoided impacts in the S1 and S2 sys- items. Similar results were found in other studies; for instance, Chen et al. (2012) identified that replacing chemical fertilizers improve environmental conditions.

Considering high-avoided impacts registered for this category, negative net impacts were obtained for the four systems evaluated, which means environmental benefits. The conventional system (S4) had the greatest benefits of -8.12E-5 kg Sb eq due to the greater amount of liquid and solid manure use for crop fields, avoiding the consumption of inorganic fertilizers. The S2 system had fewer impacts than the S1 due to the lower water consump- tion from the reuse of treated water in the process. For the biogas flaring system (S3), the highest impacts of -2.6E-5 kg Sb eq were obtained, as a result of water, diesel, and fossil energy use during treatment. Avoiding consumption of fossil resources and mineral fertilizer represented a great environmental benefit for this category.
The consumption of water resources during the manure man- agement with and without treatment is an important environmen- tal problem (FA0,2002) The treatment of liquid effluents and their

reuse in the process for cleaning facilities is a practice that must be carried out to reduce water consumption and, consequently, the depletion of water resources, which is currently a serious problem worldwide.

30: Partial energy production and composting (S1) exhibited the greatest environmental benefits on CC and PO; nevertheless, it generates less biogas, and thus, lower energy gain. In the case of the total energy production system (S2), it had less environmental benefits, but generate the greatest amount of energy. Although energy production has important environmental benefits as other LCA studies have reported (Bacenetti et al., 2013), some scientific research emphasize that biogas utilization for energy production is as important as digestate reuse or nutrient recovery (Chen et al., 2012; Loyon et al., 2016).

In both systems with energy production, important environ- mental benefits were generated by the substitution of the electric- ity consumption on the CC and FRD categories. Similar results have been found in other studies, where AD with energy recovery com- pared with conventional management scenarios was evaluated (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Croxatto et al., 2014; McAuliffe et al., 2016). The avoided emissions by energy consumption from the network depend strongly on the mix of sources used for its produc- tion. In Mexico, 80% of the energy is still generated by conventional technologies such as gas, coal and oil (SENER, 2017).

The study showed that biogas flaring (S3) is a practice that pro- duced greater environmental effects in all the evaluated categories, than the processes that use biogas for partial energy production (S1), of which FRD was the one with the greatest difference due to the electricity consumption from the national grid. Although biogas flaring is a practice with greater environmental impacts, Vu et al. (2015b) identified that in developing countries and small-scale farms biogas burning it is the best option to avoid intentional releases due to the excess of biogas produced in diges- ters. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that intentional releases represent the greatest problem during the operation of digesters, generating strong impacts on CC (Bruun et al., 2014; Vu et al., 2015b).

The conventional management (S4) exhibited the greatest envi- ronmental impacts on CC and PO compared to the other three sys- items, as well as strong impacts on the eutrophication of natural resources. Several studies have shown the importance to reduce environmental impacts avoiding the discharge of manure or liquid effluents (Luo et al., 2014; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015); however, in some countries manure or digestate are still discharged to nearby rivers (Vu et al., 2015b).

33: In the scenario with the lowest values of non-intentional and digestate storage emissions, net impacts on CC are obtained in the systems with energy production (S1 and S2) of 138 and 128 kg CO2eq, respectively (Fig. 3a). These results brought up a decrease of more than 50% of the total CO2eq compared to the orig- inal scenarios; meanwhile biogas flaring (S3) reduced impacts by 40% to 217 kg CO2eq. These reductions were explained due to fewer emissions.

In the scenario with high values, the net impacts in the S1 and S2 systems increased notably with values up to 569 and 863 kg CO2eq, respectively, and the S3 system generated impacts of 631 kg CO2eq. This provided evidence of the effect of a poor control during AD process.
The PO category had similar results than the ones described on CC (Fig. 3b). In the low scenario, values of 0.021 and 0.014 kg

ethylene eq were registered for the first two systems; while for the S3 system (biogas flaring), the impacts tripled the values obtained up to 0.064 ethylene eq. In the high scenario, the S2 system presented greater impacts of 0.176 kg ethylene eq than flaring biogas (S3) with impacts of 0.147 kg ethylene eq. Results on PO with high values confirmed the negative effects of the CH4 emissions increasing.

The conventional management system (S4) showed an increase in the two impact categories under the only scenario evaluated, for CC of 518 kg CO2eq and for PO of 0.112 kg ethylene eq. This is due to the storage of a greater amount of waste in the uncovered lagoons. When comparing the S4 system with the other three sys- tems in the low scenario, it is observed that conventional manure management generates greater impacts. However, if they are com- pared in the high scenario, the manure application in crop fields is a practice that has fewer impacts than treating the manure for energy production.

The impacts on eutrophication are observed mainly in the sys- tem with conventional management because the scenario avoids the discharge to water bodies, and thus, impacts are reduced to 1.87 kg of PO4eq (Fig. 3c). This value is the lowest compared to the impacts in the other three systems with values between 2.2 and 2.3 kg of PO4eq. In the acidification category, the S4 system under the single scenario exhibited an increase of 8.58 kg of SO2eq; this impact is lower than the net impacts (9.7 to 11.1 kg of SO2eq) generated by the other three systems (Fig. 3d).

The scenario with lower values increased the benefits on FRD in the systems S1 and S2, replacing a larger amount of fossil fuels with benefits of -962 and -1780M (Fig. 3e); this was in response to a greatest amount of biogas converted to energy. In the high scenario, with more biogas leaks and, therefore, less energy, the partial energy production system (S1) reached a slight benefit of and the S2 system reduced its benefits to -1322 MJ. The biogas flaring system showed no changes since it did not generate energy, and therefore, had the same impacts without any benefit. The conventional system presented small changes in response to lower electricity consumption; and it had greater impacts (of than the systems that produce energy (S1 and S2).

The three systems with manure treatment (S1, S2 and S3) exhibited a low uncertainty on the ARD in the two evaluated sce- narios. The abiotic resources consumption was not significantly altered in the process, as well as in the fertilizers and energy sub- stitution. The conventional S4 system reported the greatest bene- fits of -1.13E-4 kg Sb eq by avoiding the inorganic fertilizers consumption through the use of all liquid and solid manure in the crop fields (Fig.

Sensitivity analysis results obtained in this study provide evi- dence that pig manure treatment at medium-scale could also gen- erates important environmental burden on CC and PO as results of bad operational practices; as it has been reported at small-scale biogas digesters by Bruun et al. (2014) and Roubík et al. (2016). Some studies did not analyze the impacts of biogas leaks and byproducts handling, because they are carried out in farms or plants operated appropriately and with good management prac- tices, mainly in developed countries (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). However, in developing countries such as Mexico, it is essential to consider emissions from intentional and unintentional releases since manure management is carried out in rural and industrial farms without correct operating procedures in most cases.

Based on the results of the study, produce energy from manure at medium-scale has more environmental benefits than biogas flar- ing and conventional practices use. However, it is relevant to carry out manure treatment using good control practices during AD pro- cess and proper technologies during the treatment of digestate and its effluents in order to avoid higher impacts on global warming

34: In the scenario with the lowest values of non-intentional and digestate storage emissions, net impacts on CC are obtained in the systems with energy production (S1 and S2) of 138 and 128 kg CO2eq, respectively (Fig. 3a). These results brought up a decrease of more than 50% of the total CO2eq compared to the orig- inal scenarios; meanwhile biogas flaring (S3) reduced impacts by 40% to 217 kg CO2eq. These reductions were explained due to fewer emissions.

In the scenario with high values, the net impacts in the S1 and S2 systems increased notably with values up to 569 and 863 kg CO2eq, respectively, and the S3 system generated impacts of 631 kg CO2eq. This provided evidence of the effect of a poor control during AD process.
The PO category had similar results than the ones described on CC (Fig. 3b). In the low scenario, values of 0.021 and 0.014 kg

ethylene eq were registered for the first two systems; while for the S3 system (biogas flaring), the impacts tripled the values obtained up to 0.064 ethylene eq. In the high scenario, the S2 system presented greater impacts of 0.176 kg ethylene eq than flaring biogas (S3) with impacts of 0.147 kg ethylene eq. Results on PO with high values confirmed the negative effects of the CH4 emissions increasing.

The conventional management system (S4) showed an increase in the two impact categories under the only scenario evaluated, for CC of 518 kg CO2eq and for PO of 0.112 kg ethylene eq. This is due to the storage of a greater amount of waste in the uncovered lagoons. When comparing the S4 system with the other three sys- tems in the low scenario, it is observed that conventional manure management generates greater impacts. However, if they are com- pared in the high scenario, the manure application in crop fields is a practice that has fewer impacts than treating the manure for energy production.

The impacts on eutrophication are observed mainly in the sys- tem with conventional management because the scenario avoids the discharge to water bodies, and thus, impacts are reduced to 1.87 kg of PO4eq (Fig. 3c). This value is the lowest compared to the impacts in the other three systems with values between 2.2 and 2.3 kg of PO4eq. In the acidification category, the S4 system under the single scenario exhibited an increase of 8.58 kg of SO2eq; this impact is lower than the net impacts (9.7 to 11.1 kg of SO2eq) generated by the other three systems (Fig. 3d).

The scenario with lower values increased the benefits on FRD in the systems S1 and S2, replacing a larger amount of fossil fuels with benefits of -962 and -1780M (Fig. 3e); this was in response to a greatest amount of biogas converted to energy. In the high scenario, with more biogas leaks and, therefore, less energy, the partial energy production system (S1) reached a slight benefit of and the S2 system reduced its benefits to -1322 MJ. The biogas flaring system showed no changes since it did not generate energy, and therefore, had the same impacts without any benefit. The conventional system presented small changes in response to lower electricity consumption; and it had greater impacts (of than the systems that produce energy (S1 and S2).

The three systems with manure treatment (S1, S2 and S3) exhibited a low uncertainty on the ARD in the two evaluated sce- narios. The abiotic resources consumption was not significantly altered in the process, as well as in the fertilizers and energy sub- stitution. The conventional S4 system reported the greatest bene- fits of -1.13E-4 kg Sb eq by avoiding the inorganic fertilizers consumption through the use of all liquid and solid manure in the crop fields (Fig.

Sensitivity analysis results obtained in this study provide evi- dence that pig manure treatment at medium-scale could also gen- erates important environmental burden on CC and PO as results of bad operational practices; as it has been reported at small-scale biogas digesters by Bruun et al. (2014) and Roubík et al. (2016). Some studies did not analyze the impacts of biogas leaks and byproducts handling, because they are carried out in farms or plants operated appropriately and with good management prac- tices, mainly in developed countries (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). However, in developing countries such as Mexico, it is essential to consider emissions from intentional and unintentional releases since manure management is carried out in rural and industrial farms without correct operating procedures in most cases.

Based on the results of the study, produce energy from manure at medium-scale has more environmental benefits than biogas flar- ing and conventional practices use. However, it is relevant to carry out manure treatment using good control practices during AD pro- cess and proper technologies during the treatment of digestate and its effluents in order to avoid higher impacts on global warming

(2014) and Roubík et al. (2016). Some studies did not analyze the impacts of biogas leaks and byproducts handling, because they are carried out in farms or plants operated appropriately and with good management prac- tices, mainly in developed countries (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). However, in developing countries such as Mexico, it is essential to consider emissions from intentional and unintentional releases since manure management is carried out in rural and industrial farms without correct operating procedures in most cases.

Based on the results of the study, produce energy from manure at medium-scale has more environmental benefits than biogas flar- ing and conventional practices use. However, it is relevant to carry out manure treatment using good control practices during AD pro- cess and proper technologies during the treatment of digestate and its effluents in order to avoid higher impacts on global warming

38: In the scenario with the lowest values of non-intentional and digestate storage emissions, net impacts on CC are obtained in the systems with energy production (S1 and S2) of 138 and 128 kg CO2eq, respectively (Fig. 3a). These results brought up a decrease of more than 50% of the total CO2eq compared to the orig- inal scenarios; meanwhile biogas flaring (S3) reduced impacts by 40% to 217 kg CO2eq. These reductions were explained due to fewer emissions.

In the scenario with high values, the net impacts in the S1 and S2 systems increased notably with values up to 569 and 863 kg CO2eq, respectively, and the S3 system generated impacts of 631 kg CO2eq. This provided evidence of the effect of a poor control during AD process.
The PO category had similar results than the ones described on CC (Fig. 3b). In the low scenario, values of 0.021 and 0.014 kg

ethylene eq were registered for the first two systems; while for the S3 system (biogas flaring), the impacts tripled the values obtained up to 0.064 ethylene eq. In the high scenario, the S2 system presented greater impacts of 0.176 kg ethylene eq than flaring biogas (S3) with impacts of 0.147 kg ethylene eq. Results on PO with high values confirmed the negative effects of the CH4 emissions increasing.

The conventional management system (S4) showed an increase in the two impact categories under the only scenario evaluated, for CC of 518 kg CO2eq and for PO of 0.112 kg ethylene eq. This is due to the storage of a greater amount of waste in the uncovered lagoons. When comparing the S4 system with the other three sys- tems in the low scenario, it is observed that conventional manure management generates greater impacts. However, if they are com- pared in the high scenario, the manure application in crop fields is a practice that has fewer impacts than treating the manure for energy production.

The impacts on eutrophication are observed mainly in the sys- tem with conventional management because the scenario avoids the discharge to water bodies, and thus, impacts are reduced to 1.87 kg of PO4eq (Fig. 3c). This value is the lowest compared to the impacts in the other three systems with values between 2.2 and 2.3 kg of PO4eq. In the acidification category, the S4 system under the single scenario exhibited an increase of 8.58 kg of SO2eq; this impact is lower than the net impacts (9.7 to 11.1 kg of SO2eq) generated by the other three systems (Fig. 3d).

The scenario with lower values increased the benefits on FRD in the systems S1 and S2, replacing a larger amount of fossil fuels with benefits of -962 and -1780M (Fig. 3e); this was in response to a greatest amount of biogas converted to energy. In the high scenario, with more biogas leaks and, therefore, less energy, the partial energy production system (S1) reached a slight benefit of and the S2 system reduced its benefits to -1322 MJ. The biogas flaring system showed no changes since it did not generate energy, and therefore, had the same impacts without any benefit. The conventional system presented small changes in response to lower electricity consumption; and it had greater impacts (of than the systems that produce energy (S1 and S2).

The three systems with manure treatment (S1, S2 and S3) exhibited a low uncertainty on the ARD in the two evaluated sce- narios. The abiotic resources consumption was not significantly altered in the process, as well as in the fertilizers and energy sub- stitution. The conventional S4 system reported the greatest bene- fits of -1.13E-4 kg Sb eq by avoiding the inorganic fertilizers consumption through the use of all liquid and solid manure in the crop fields (Fig.

The biogas flaring system showed no changes since it did not generate energy, and therefore, had the same impacts without any benefit. The conventional system presented small changes in response to lower electricity consumption; and it had greater impacts (of than the systems that produce energy (S1 and S2).

The three systems with manure treatment (S1, S2 and S3) exhibited a low uncertainty on the ARD in the two evaluated sce- narios. The abiotic resources consumption was not significantly altered in the process, as well as in the fertilizers and energy sub- stitution. The conventional S4 system reported the greatest bene- fits of -1.13E-4 kg Sb eq by avoiding the inorganic fertilizers consumption through the use of all liquid and solid manure in the crop fields (Fig.

Sensitivity analysis results obtained in this study provide evi- dence that pig manure treatment at medium-scale could also gen- erates important environmental burden on CC and PO as results of bad operational practices; as it has been reported at small-scale biogas digesters by Bruun et al. (2014) and Roubík et al. (2016). Some studies did not analyze the impacts of biogas leaks and byproducts handling, because they are carried out in farms or plants operated appropriately and with good management prac- tices, mainly in developed countries (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). However, in developing countries such as Mexico, it is essential to consider emissions from intentional and unintentional releases since manure management is carried out in rural and industrial farms without correct operating procedures in most cases.

Based on the results of the study, produce energy from manure at medium-scale has more environmental benefits than biogas flar- ing and conventional practices use. However, it is relevant to carry out manure treatment using good control practices during AD pro- cess and proper technologies during the treatment of digestate and its effluents in order to avoid higher impacts on global warming

42: In the scenario with the lowest values of non-intentional and digestate storage emissions, net impacts on CC are obtained in the systems with energy production (S1 and S2) of 138 and 128 kg CO2eq, respectively (Fig. 3a). These results brought up a decrease of more than 50% of the total CO2eq compared to the orig- inal scenarios; meanwhile biogas flaring (S3) reduced impacts by 40% to 217 kg CO2eq. These reductions were explained due to fewer emissions.

In the scenario with high values, the net impacts in the S1 and S2 systems increased notably with values up to 569 and 863 kg CO2eq, respectively, and the S3 system generated impacts of 631 kg CO2eq. This provided evidence of the effect of a poor control during AD process.
The PO category had similar results than the ones described on CC (Fig. 3b). In the low scenario, values of 0.021 and 0.014 kg

ethylene eq were registered for the first two systems; while for the S3 system (biogas flaring), the impacts tripled the values obtained up to 0.064 ethylene eq. In the high scenario, the S2 system presented greater impacts of 0.176 kg ethylene eq than flaring biogas (S3) with impacts of 0.147 kg ethylene eq. Results on PO with high values confirmed the negative effects of the CH4 emissions increasing.

The conventional management system (S4) showed an increase in the two impact categories under the only scenario evaluated, for CC of 518 kg CO2eq and for PO of 0.112 kg ethylene eq. This is due to the storage of a greater amount of waste in the uncovered lagoons. When comparing the S4 system with the other three sys- tems in the low scenario, it is observed that conventional manure management generates greater impacts. However, if they are com- pared in the high scenario, the manure application in crop fields is a practice that has fewer impacts than treating the manure for energy production.

The impacts on eutrophication are observed mainly in the sys- tem with conventional management because the scenario avoids the discharge to water bodies, and thus, impacts are reduced to 1.87 kg of PO4eq (Fig. 3c). This value is the lowest compared to the impacts in the other three systems with values between 2.2 and 2.3 kg of PO4eq. In the acidification category, the S4 system under the single scenario exhibited an increase of 8.58 kg of SO2eq; this impact is lower than the net impacts (9.7 to 11.1 kg of SO2eq) generated by the other three systems (Fig. 3d).

practices to achieve a sustainable manure management. Economic assessment studies in both rural (Surendra et al., 2014; Yasar et al., 2017) and full-scale applications (Renda et al., 2016; De Vrieze et al., 2019) have been conducted; nevertheless, their results are not conclusive since the economic feasibility of manure manage- ment practices depends on diverse factors, including technical, economic and social conditions in the countries implemented.

The study performed a preliminary estimation of benefits as well as operational and maintenance costs (CO&M) per ton of man- ure for the four evaluated systems (Table 6). Results indicated that the two systems with energy production had the highest costs, but also the highest economic benefits due to savings for electricity self-consumption. Hence, energy production systems have more environmental and economic benefits than biogas flaring; how- ever, a detailed economic analysis including the environmental and social costs, are necessary for an improved assessment.

practices to achieve a sustainable manure management. Economic assessment studies in both rural (Surendra et al., 2014; Yasar et al., 2017) and full-scale applications (Renda et al., 2016; De Vrieze et al., 2019) have been conducted; nevertheless, their results are not conclusive since the economic feasibility of manure manage- ment practices depends on diverse factors, including technical, economic and social conditions in the countries implemented.

The study performed a preliminary estimation of benefits as well as operational and maintenance costs (CO&M) per ton of man- ure for the four evaluated systems (Table 6). Results indicated that the two systems with energy production had the highest costs, but also the highest economic benefits due to savings for electricity self-consumption. Hence, energy production systems have more environmental and economic benefits than biogas flaring; how- ever, a detailed economic analysis including the environmental and social costs, are necessary for an improved assessment.
45: Based on the LCA of the study conducted, the energy production of pig manure by anaerobic digestion at medium-scale is a technol- ogy that proved important environmental benefits on CC, PO, and FRD. The manure treatment using AD and composting techniques had the best environmental profile, reducing emissions by 130 kg CO2eq and avoiding the use of 729 MJ per ton of treated manure, compared to the conventional system.

Flaring biogas instead of producing energy has a greater envi- ronmental burden, increasing by 26% the impacts on CC, 70% on PO, and 10% on AC. This practice, commonly used in developing countries, contributes to FRD because biogas is flared and savings energy consumption is not generated.
